joke.raw_joke: A dog limps into a saloon in the old west — And says “I’m lookin’ for the man who shot my paw”

blocks.content:
### seed-173

**MILO_OFFICIAL_SCRIPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-173
edit_level: pass-through

script:
- setup: "A dog limps into a saloon in the Old West."
- punchline: "Says: 'I'm lookin' for the man who shot my paw.'"
- tag: ~

changes_made: Minimal — split the raw into two clean beats (scene-set, then the line) and standardized punctuation. The original had it in one run-on sentence; splitting it gives the audience the visual beat of a limping dog before the dialogue lands. The "lookin'" contraction stays — it's doing accent work.

caption_notes: Two cards. Setup: "A dog limps into a saloon in the Old West." Punchline: "I'm lookin' for the man who shot my paw." — the punchline card is the entire joke. The apostrophe in "lookin'" should render correctly in caption formatting. "Paw" in isolation on its own card at the end of the line is where the double meaning lands — do not bold or highlight it, that telegraphs the joke.

timing_notes: Setup delivered at normal narration pace. 0.4s pause — give the audience time to picture the limping dog and assume this is a wounded-animal revenge story. Punchline in a slight Western drawl if TTS supports register adjustment; if not, flat delivery is fine. The joke parses on the second hearing without any vocal assistance. Do not stress "paw."

tts_notes: "paw" — one syllable, rhymes with "pa." No pronunciation issue, but if TTS adds any extra stress or elongation to "paw," the wordplay becomes too obvious too early. Flat, matter-of-fact delivery of the whole sentence including the final word.

quality_flag: strong

---

### seed-173

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Saloon Bartender / Scene Witness | Vince Clip — Bartender persona | Vince is the grounded human anchor in a scene that's otherwise pure absurdity. His "process realist / scene stabilizer" archetype puts him behind the bar watching this happen with the flat expression of a man who has seen some things, and this ranks among them. His deadpan is the visual punchline register — the dog delivers the line, Vince receives it. |
| The Dog | Animated cartoon character — no cast anchor | The dog cannot be mapped to an existing cast member. This is a distinct illustrated character: cartoon western dog, wide-brimmed hat, bandana, visible limp on a rear leg. Designed by Iris as a one-off character for this concept only. See adaptation notes. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Vince: Western bartender wardrobe — collarless shirt, suspenders, bar apron. No camera equipment. His wild spiky black hair and rectangular glasses remain visible under or with the period-appropriate look. He is behind a wooden saloon bar, polishing a glass when the dog enters. He does not stop polishing the glass during the dog's line. He just watches.
- The Dog (Iris direction): Cartoon illustration, quadruped, wearing a small wide-brimmed cowboy hat canted at a slight angle, a neckerchief, and a holster-and-belt arrangement that implies this dog came prepared. The rear left leg is visibly bent or raised — the limp is clear but not graphic. The dog stands in the saloon doorway with afternoon sun backlighting it. Expression: determined, weathered, looking for justice. Give it a slight squint. This is a dog with a mission.
- Setting: Old West saloon interior — wooden bar, bottles on shelves behind it, sawdust floor, swinging saloon doors. The dog pushes through the swinging doors in the hook shot. Afternoon light floods in from outside.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Establish the saloon interior — Vince behind the bar, other blurred patrons. The saloon doors swing open. Backlit silhouette of a small figure with a hat in the doorway. The figure limps forward into the light: it's the dog.
- **Punchline:** Close-up on the dog's determined face as it delivers the line. Cut to Vince — he has stopped polishing the glass. He is looking at the dog. His expression is the expression of a man deciding whether this counts as his problem. He decides it does not. He resumes polishing the glass.

---

### seed-173

**IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-173
concepts_count: 3

Milo script: ✅ Received. Setup: "A dog limps into a saloon in the Old West." Punchline: "Says: 'I'm lookin' for the man who shot my paw.'" "Lookin'" contraction stays — accent work. Don't stress "paw."
Riley casting honored: Vince Clip (Bartender persona, Western wardrobe) as saloon bartender/witness. The Dog is a one-off illustrated character — quadruped, cowboy hat, bandana, holster, visible limp on rear left leg. Iris designs.

**Surreal flag: EARNED.** A talking dog in a Western saloon delivering a revenge line is already absurd, but the pun "paw/pa" begs for a literal visual — the dog's actual paw bandaged, the audience reading both meanings simultaneously. Concept C goes surreal.

---

**Concept A — "The Saloon Doors"**
Classic Western entrance. Doors swing, backlit silhouette, the dog limps in. Vince behind the bar watches it happen. Played completely straight.
Duration: 9.4s — The door-swing entrance needs visual breathing room, and Vince's non-reaction in Scene 4 is the payoff that earns the hold.

Master video prompt:
"Old West saloon interior, cartoon illustration 2.5D, clean bold lines, warm amber-and-dust lighting, vertical 9:16 portrait. Wooden bar along the right side, shelves of bottles behind it. Sawdust floor. Swinging saloon doors at the back center. Vince Clip (wild spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, goatee) in Western bartender wardrobe — collarless shirt, suspenders, bar apron — standing behind the bar polishing a glass. A cartoon dog character — medium-sized, scruffy brown fur, wearing a small wide-brimmed cowboy hat tilted slightly, red bandana around neck, a tiny gun belt and holster around its midsection — limps through the saloon doors. Rear left leg visibly raised/bent. Afternoon sunlight floods in from outside through the doors. Caption-safe lower 20% clear."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.4s): HOOK — THE DOORS
An Old West saloon interior — dark wooden bar along the right side of the frame, shelves of dusty bottles behind it (whiskey, bourbon, labels illegible), a brass rail along the bar's foot. The floor is rough wood with sawdust scattered. A few blurred patron silhouettes sit at tables in the background. At the back of the room, a pair of wooden swinging saloon doors are pushed inward — they swing open, afternoon golden sunlight flooding through the gap in a wedge of light across the floor. Standing in the doorway, backlit by bright afternoon sun, a small silhouette: a four-legged figure wearing a hat. The figure is low to the ground, clearly not human. It limps forward into the saloon — one rear leg (the left) is held up slightly, not bearing full weight. As it steps out of the backlight and into the dim saloon interior, the figure resolves: a cartoon dog. Scruffy brown fur, medium-sized, wearing a small wide-brimmed cowboy hat tilted at a slight angle on its head, a red bandana knotted around its neck, and a tiny leather gun belt with a small holster buckled around its midsection. The dog's eyes are narrowed in a determined squint — this is not a pet. This is a dog with a mission. Behind the bar, Vince Clip — a man with wild spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, and a goatee — wears a collarless white shirt, dark suspenders, and a bar apron. He holds a glass and a polishing cloth. He has looked up from the glass. He is watching the door.

Scene 2 (2.4–5.4s): SETUP — THE LIMP
Same saloon interior. The dog (scruffy brown fur, cowboy hat tilted, red bandana, gun belt with holster, rear left leg raised) has moved further into the saloon from the doorway. The swinging doors behind it are settling. Afternoon light still streams through the gap. The dog stands in the middle of the saloon floor, sawdust under its three weight-bearing paws. Its rear left leg is clearly raised — bent at the joint, not touching the ground. This is a visible, unambiguous limp. The dog's expression is steady, squinting, determined — a weathered traveler who has come a long way for a specific purpose. It faces into the room, looking past the viewer toward the bar. Behind the bar, Vince Clip (spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, goatee, suspenders, bar apron) has not stopped polishing the glass. He is watching the dog with the flat, appraising expression of a bartender who has decided this is happening and has not yet decided what to do about it.
Caption: "A dog limps into a saloon in the Old West."

Scene 3 (5.4–5.8s): BEAT
Same saloon. Closer framing on the dog (cowboy hat, bandana, gun belt, raised rear leg). The dog's jaw sets. Its squint deepens. It is about to speak. Behind it, the saloon doors are still. The room is quiet.

Scene 4 (5.8–9.4s): PUNCHLINE — THE LINE
Same saloon interior. The dog (scruffy brown fur, cowboy hat, red bandana, gun belt, rear left leg raised off the ground) stands on the sawdust floor, facing toward the bar. Its mouth is slightly open, delivering its line. Its expression is dead serious — a protagonist making a declaration. This dog has been traveling. This dog has a grievance. This dog is not leaving until the grievance is addressed. Behind the bar, Vince Clip (spiky hair, glasses, goatee, suspenders, apron) has stopped polishing the glass. The glass is in one hand, the cloth in the other, both motionless at waist height. He is looking at the dog. His expression is the expression of a man calculating whether this is his problem. He has run the calculation. It is not his problem. But he is still holding the glass. He has not resumed polishing. The bottles on the shelves behind him catch the dim amber light. The saloon is silent except for the dog's declaration. The swinging doors are still. The afternoon light through the gap has dimmed slightly. Nobody else in the saloon has moved.
Caption: "'I'm lookin' for the man who shot my paw.'"

Caption plan: 0.0–2.4s silent | 2.4–5.4s setup | 5.8–9.4s punchline. Apostrophe in "lookin'" must render. Do not bold "paw."
Voice: Setup as third-person narration — warm, storytelling register, Western ambiance. 0.4s pause. Punchline in a slight drawl if TTS supports register — the dog's voice. If not, flat narration quoting the dog's line. Do not stress "paw." The word sits at the end of the sentence and carries both meanings; the audience catches it.
Risk: LOW-MEDIUM. The dog is a one-off character design — needs to render consistently across 4 scenes (hat, bandana, gun belt, raised leg). Cartoon 2.5D style helps — stylized animals are more forgiving than realistic ones. Vince in Western wardrobe is a costume change but straightforward.
Fallback: Simplify the dog to just scruffy brown fur + cowboy hat + raised rear leg. Drop the gun belt and bandana if the AI gen can't handle all the details. The hat and the limp are the non-negotiable elements.
Jerry hook: "The backlit silhouette entrance through swinging saloon doors — the audience sees a small hatted figure and doesn't know it's a dog until it limps into the light. That two-second mystery is the hook. Vince stopping mid-polish and deciding this isn't his problem is the visual button."
Vince handoff: aspectRatio 9:16 | durationSec 9.4 | sceneCount 4 | mustKeep [Scene 1: backlit door entrance + dog silhouette reveal, Scene 4: dog delivering line + Vince frozen mid-polish] | trims [Scene 3 can cut to 0.2s]

---

**Concept B — "The Bartender's Perspective"**
Vince is the anchor. Opens on him behind the bar. The dog enters in the background. Vince's non-reaction IS the concept — he watches the whole thing happen and says nothing.
Duration: 8.8s — Vince-centric framing is tighter because the comedy is in his face, not the dog's entrance.

Master video prompt:
"Old West saloon interior, cartoon illustration 2.5D, clean bold lines, warm amber lighting, vertical 9:16 portrait. Framing: Vince Clip (spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, goatee, collarless shirt, suspenders, bar apron) behind a wooden bar, polishing a glass. He dominates the upper two-thirds of the frame. In the background: swinging saloon doors, blurred patrons. A cartoon dog (scruffy brown fur, cowboy hat, red bandana, gun belt, limping rear left leg) enters through the doors and approaches the bar. Caption-safe lower 20% clear."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — THE BARTENDER
An Old West saloon — viewed from the customer side of a long wooden bar. Behind the bar stands Vince Clip — wild spiky black hair, rectangular glasses, neatly trimmed goatee, wearing a collarless white shirt with the sleeves rolled to the elbows, dark suspenders, a bar apron tied at the waist. He holds a glass in one hand and a polishing cloth in the other, methodically wiping. His expression is the default Vince: flat, unbothered, a man whose shift has been uneventful. Shelves of dusty bottles line the wall behind him. The bar surface is dark wood, slightly worn. In the background past Vince's shoulder, at the far end of the room, the swinging saloon doors begin to move — pushed inward by something low to the ground. Afternoon light starts to spill through the gap.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.0s): SETUP — THE ARRIVAL
Same saloon, same framing on Vince behind the bar. His eyes have shifted — he is looking past the viewer, toward the saloon floor in front of the bar. His polishing has slowed but not stopped. Something has entered the room. In the lower portion of the frame, approaching the bar from the background, a cartoon dog limps forward — scruffy brown fur, a small wide-brimmed cowboy hat on its head, a red bandana around its neck, a tiny gun belt and holster around its middle. Its rear left leg is held up, not touching the ground. The dog moves with purpose across the sawdust floor, heading toward the bar. It stops in front of the bar, below Vince's line of sight. Vince looks down at the dog. His polishing hand has stopped moving. His expression has not changed — it is still flat, still appraising. He is watching a limping dog in a cowboy hat approach his bar. He is processing this at his own pace.
Caption: "A dog limps into a saloon in the Old West."

Scene 3 (5.0–5.3s): BEAT
Same framing — Vince behind the bar, looking down. The dog (hat, bandana, gun belt, raised leg) stands at the base of the bar, looking up. Two characters making eye contact across a vertical height difference that fills the 9:16 frame naturally.

Scene 4 (5.3–8.8s): PUNCHLINE
Same saloon, same framing. The dog (scruffy brown fur, cowboy hat, red bandana, gun belt, raised rear leg) stands at the base of the bar, looking upward with narrowed determined eyes. Its mouth is open slightly, delivering its line with gravel and purpose. It is making a declaration to the room. Above and behind the bar, Vince Clip (spiky hair, glasses, goatee, suspenders, apron) looks down at the dog. He has stopped polishing entirely. The glass and cloth hang at his sides. His expression is perfectly flat — the face of a man who has received information and is choosing not to act on it. He does not look surprised. He does not look amused. He looks like a man who will resume polishing the glass in approximately three seconds and never mention this again. The bottles on the shelves glint. The saloon is quiet.
Caption: "'I'm lookin' for the man who shot my paw.'"

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s silent | 2.0–5.0s setup | 5.3–8.8s punchline.
Voice: Same as Concept A. Narration + dog quote.
Risk: LOW. Same character design requirements but the framing keeps Vince dominant and the dog smaller in frame — fewer details to render on the dog at smaller scale.
Fallback: If the dog renders poorly at small scale, zoom framing so it fills more of the lower frame. Or simplify to hat + raised leg only.
Jerry hook: "This is the 'reaction video' version — the comedy is in Vince's face, not the dog's entrance. The Vince-behind-the-bar anchor means the audience has a human to read emotionally while the absurd thing happens below. His decision to not react is the punchline's visual partner."
Vince handoff: aspectRatio 9:16 | durationSec 8.8 | sceneCount 4 | mustKeep [Scene 4: dog delivering line + Vince's flat non-reaction looking down, Scene 2: dog limping toward bar + Vince noticing] | trims [Scene 3 can cut to 0.15s]

---

**Concept C [SURREAL] — "The Paw"**
Grounded Western saloon setup — then the camera drops to the dog's injured paw, and the paw is bandaged with a tiny framed photograph tucked into the bandage: a photo of an older dog wearing a cowboy hat. Pa. Paw. Both meanings visible in the same frame.
Duration: 10.0s — The surreal reveal in Scene 4 needs screen time to register.

surreal_element: The dog's injured rear paw has a small white bandage wrapped around it. Tucked into the bandage, like a soldier carrying a photo into battle, is a tiny framed photograph of an older dog — gray-muzzled, wearing a larger cowboy hat, dignified. This is the dog's pa. The injured paw AND the father are visible in the same frame. The word "paw" means both things simultaneously, and the visual makes both meanings literal.

Transformation beat: 7.2–10.0s — not a transformation of reality but a surreal visual reveal. The camera framing drops to the dog's paw level. The bandaged paw is center frame. The tiny photograph of the father-dog is tucked into the bandage wrapping. The audience sees a wounded paw containing a portrait of a pa. Both meanings coexist in a single image. This is not how photos work. This is not how bandages work. It doesn't matter.

Weirdness budget: 4/10 — The surreal element is contained and specific. It's not melting physics or impossible scale. It's one impossible detail (a photo tucked into a paw bandage) in an otherwise grounded scene. Gentle weird. Heartfelt weird.

Master video prompt:
"Old West saloon interior, cartoon illustration 2.5D, clean bold lines, warm amber lighting, vertical 9:16 portrait. Standard saloon setup with Vince behind bar. Cartoon dog (scruffy brown fur, cowboy hat, bandana, gun belt) in doorway. SURREAL ELEMENT: The dog's rear left paw has a small white bandage, and tucked into the bandage wrapping is a tiny oval-framed photograph of an older, gray-muzzled dog wearing a larger cowboy hat — dignified, paternal. Caption-safe lower 20% clear."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.2s): HOOK — GROUNDED
Same saloon setup as Concept A — wooden bar, bottle shelves, sawdust floor, swinging doors. Vince Clip (spiky hair, glasses, goatee, suspenders, bar apron) behind the bar with glass and cloth. The saloon doors swing open. Backlit by afternoon sun, a small figure with a hat limps through. The dog enters — scruffy brown fur, cowboy hat, red bandana, gun belt, rear left leg raised. It limps into the saloon. Vince watches. This scene is played completely straight — no surreal elements yet.

Scene 2 (2.2–5.2s): SETUP
Same saloon. The dog (cowboy hat, bandana, gun belt, raised rear leg) stands on the sawdust floor in the middle of the room. Its expression is determined, squinting. Behind the bar, Vince (spiky hair, glasses, goatee, suspenders) has slowed his polishing. He is watching. The dog is framed full-body — hat to paws — in the vertical frame. Its rear left leg is visibly raised, but at this distance the bandage is not the focus. The scene reads as: injured dog in a saloon. Western revenge premise.
Caption: "A dog limps into a saloon in the Old West."

Scene 3 (5.2–7.2s): THE LINE
Same saloon. The dog (cowboy hat, bandana, gun belt) faces toward the bar. Its mouth opens. Its squint deepens. It delivers the line with dead-serious determination. Behind the bar, Vince has stopped polishing. He is looking at the dog with his flat expression — the information has been received.
Caption: "'I'm lookin' for the man who shot my paw.'"

Scene 4 (7.2–10.0s): SURREAL REVEAL — THE PAW
The framing drops. We are at floor level now — the sawdust, the wooden floorboards, the base of the bar visible in the background. Center frame: the dog's rear left paw, close up. The paw is wrapped in a small white bandage — clean, neatly tied, clearly a wound that has been tended to. The paw itself is brown-furred, small, the toes visible around the bandage's edges. And tucked into the bandage wrapping, held in place by the white cloth like a locket worn into battle: a tiny oval-framed photograph. The photo shows an older dog — gray muzzle, dignified bearing, wearing a larger and more ornate cowboy hat. This dog is paternal. This dog is wise. This dog has been shot. This is the pa. This is the paw. Both meanings occupy the same two square inches of screen. The bandaged, injured paw holds the portrait of the father who was hurt. The audience sees it. Both readings activate simultaneously. The sawdust on the floor catches the light. Vince's boots are visible behind the bar in the deep background. The saloon is still.
Caption: (no additional caption — hold on the visual. The punchline caption from Scene 3 remains readable, or this scene holds in silence.)

Caption plan: 0.0–2.2s silent | 2.2–5.2s setup | 5.2–7.2s punchline | 7.2–10.0s hold on paw/pa reveal (silent or punchline caption persists).
Voice: Same narration + dog quote timing for Scenes 1–3. Scene 4 is silent visual hold — no additional VO. The image speaks. Optional: a single low note of Western score, or a wind sound. Or silence.
Risk: MEDIUM. The photo-in-bandage detail is specific and small — AI gen may not render the tiny framed photo clearly at paw-level. The photo needs to be recognizable as "an older dog" even at small size. Fallback: Skip the photo. Just show the bandaged paw in close-up — the word "paw" lands on the caption while the literal wounded paw is center frame. Less surreal, still effective. Or: make the photo larger — wallet-sized, propped against the paw on the floor rather than tucked into the bandage.
Jerry hook: "This one's weird on purpose. The AI will make it weirder. That's the bit. The pa's photo tucked into the wounded paw is an image that hits the audience with both meanings at once — it's a visual pun that you can't unhear once you see it. The tiny dignified old dog in the photograph is a character who exists for two seconds and earns more sympathy than most leads."
Vince handoff: aspectRatio 9:16 | durationSec 10.0 | sceneCount 4 | mustKeep [Scene 4: paw close-up with pa photo — this IS the concept, Scene 1: door entrance for context] | trims [Scene 2 can trim 0.5s, Scene 3 can trim 0.3s; do NOT trim Scene 4]

---

quality_gate: PASS — Three meaningfully different concepts (classic entrance, bartender-POV, surreal paw reveal), full timestamps, punchline readable, vertical framing preserved, surreal concept includes transformation beat + weirdness budget + fallback. Both cast members (Vince + dog) appear on screen in all concepts.
status: ready_for_jerry_review

---
